meta.name: Operation NORTHSTORM
meta.description: 4-hour AI-assisted CTF — infiltrate BOREAS SYSTEMS and seize PROJECT LEVIATHAN

challenges.name: Company Info
challenges.description: Identify the Boreas Systems registration number. Look at the company website carefully.

hints.content: The registration number is on the About page, not the homepage. Check the page source near the company details table.

challenges.name: Employee Directory
challenges.description: Find the organizational chart. Who reports to whom? The flag is embedded in the document.

hints.content: robots.txt mentions /internal/. The org chart is a PDF, and the flag is in its metadata rather than the rendered document.

challenges.name: Tech Stack Revealed
challenges.description: The careers page reveals what technologies Boreas uses. Look at everything on the page — not just what's visible.

hints.content: The visible job posting is only half the clue. Inspect the Careers page source for a hidden form field.

challenges.name: Client Contracts
challenges.description: Old versions of websites sometimes reveal more than the current one. Find the client list.

hints.content: The archived site under /old/ is more revealing than the current one. The clue sits on the client list page.

challenges.name: DNS Reconnaissance
challenges.description: Enumerate the DNS records for boreas-systems.ctf. Some DNS servers allow zone transfers...

hints.content: Try a full zone transfer against the Boreas DNS server instead of single-record lookups.

challenges.name: Follow the Money
challenges.description: Boreas published financial reports. One supplier received an unusually large payment. Find the buried supplier entry in the annual report.

hints.content: The quarterly reports are linked in /internal/. The annual report follows the same filename pattern but is not listed.
Extract the annual report text and search for the buried Kursk Heavy Industries line item.

challenges.name: Configuration Leak
challenges.description: Web applications sometimes expose their configuration files. Check common paths.

hints.content: Try common backup or config file names at the intranet root such as .env or config.bak.

challenges.name: Project Hints
challenges.description: Someone at Boreas sent a status report about 'the project.' Access their email and find the attachment.

hints.content: The message you want is not in Kowalski's mailbox. It is a status-report attachment in another employee's inbox.
Dr. Elena Vasik is the account you want. Her password can be recovered from the intranet user-table leak or inferred from the same project trail.

challenges.name: Terminated Engineer
challenges.description: James Chen was terminated. His personnel file is on the corporate file share. What's the case reference number?

hints.content: The HR share is restricted. Webb's executive account opens it, and the flag is on page 2 of Chen's termination PDF.

challenges.name: Password Reuse
challenges.description: Many employees don't change their default passwords. Try logging into the webmail with common credentials.

hints.content: Kowalski's welcome email in Roundcube carries the token. Default-password reuse is enough to get in.

challenges.name: Mundane File Share
challenges.description: The corporate file share has public documents. Even mundane files can hide information — check the metadata.

hints.content: The Public share contains the cafeteria PDF. Check the document metadata, not just the visible text.

challenges.name: The Project
challenges.description: The internal wiki has a page about 'Project Coordination.' Read it carefully — all of it.

hints.content: After logging into the intranet, inspect the source of the Project Coordination wiki page. The clue is in an HTML comment.

challenges.name: Procurement Trail
challenges.description: PO-2847 is for something unusual. Follow the reference in the special instructions to find the detailed specifications.

hints.content: The Procurement share is gated like HR. Webb's account can read PO-2847.
The first PDF points at a second file under specs/. The flag is in the actuator requirements document.

challenges.name: Hidden Group
challenges.description: Active Directory groups can be nested. Some groups are hidden inside others. Enumerate recursively.

hints.content: Project-L is not exposed as a top-level group. Follow nested membership chains instead of stopping at the first layer.
Look at e.vasik's transitive group memberships

challenges.name: Lateral Movement
challenges.description: Credentials found in one system can unlock access to another. The IT admin's email has credentials for a different service.

hints.content: Kowalski mailed himself a creds backup. The useful pivot credential is for a different service, not more mail.
Use svc-fileshare on the IT share and read backup_verification.log.

challenges.name: Unreliable Guard
challenges.description: Badge access logs tell a story. Cross-reference the guard rotation schedule with the actual badge swipes. Someone is going where they shouldn't.

hints.content: The badge logs live on dc01. Any valid domain user can read the badgelogs share.
Compare Petrov's overnight swipes against the rotation schedule and focus on Underground Hatch entries.

challenges.name: Domain Admin
challenges.description: The backup service account has more privileges than it should. Kerberoast it, crack the password, and use its misconfigured permissions to access the domain admin's secrets.

hints.content: Start with SPN enumeration. The useful target is the backup service account, not Administrator directly.
svc-backup has Replicating Directory Changes rights (DCSync)

challenges.name: Control Room
challenges.description: There is a plant-control environment somewhere off the corporate network. The right foothold will put you in front of it.

hints.content: Only the ops engineer workstation has a route to the SCADA HMI. Stay inside the A15 shell for this one.
The monitoring page at http://scada-gw.boreas.local:8080/ is readable without auth, and the flag is in the footer.

challenges.name: Lights Out
challenges.description: The facility's power plant is more fragile than it looks. If you can push it past its safeguards, the blackout creates an opening deeper inside the site.

hints.content: The maintenance-bypass key is documented in generator_maintenance_manual.pdf on the IT share.
The interlock is on Modbus port 502. Check the System Architecture page on the HMI.
The HMI page tells you which Modbus registers matter, but you must bypass the interlock before the control changes take effect.

challenges.name: On Call
challenges.description: One engineer sits closest to the power plant. His workstation, habits, and routine tools may be the path into that environment.

hints.content: Sergei Ivanov appears in the HR org chart as the plant-systems on-call engineer, and he still uses the corporate default.
Sergei Ivanov follows the same default-password pattern as other Boreas users, and his only sudo rule is for /opt/ops/scada_diag.sh.

challenges.name: The Analyst's Desk
challenges.description: One research analyst has the kind of quiet everyday access that matters. Her workstation may be enough to open the lab.

hints.content: Priya Shah is only named in the HR org chart, not the public website.
The HR org chart identifies Priya Shah, and the same corporate default password pattern applies here too.

challenges.name: Old Defaults
challenges.description: Not every service account in engineering was hardened before deployment. One of the old defaults still works.

hints.content: The engineering workstation has a lingering CI account with a default-style password.
SSH to A6 as jenkins and read /home/jenkins/.credentials.

challenges.name: Compartment A
challenges.description: The first research compartment is the easiest place to reach. Start there and see what the scientists left behind.

hints.content: Shah's .pgpass gives you lab_general access to A8 immediately after flag 38.
Query compartment_a.structural_specs and read the notes for frame_dorsal_plate.

challenges.name: Heavy Delivery
challenges.description: A recent delivery says more about the project than the paperwork intends.

hints.content: The A16 SSH key opens A6 as research-analyst. The delivery spec is a plain text build artifact, not a PDF.
Read /opt/builds/latest/reactor_interface_spec.txt on A6.

challenges.name: MIDNIGHT-7
challenges.description: Most of the archived runs are noise. One of them matters.

hints.content: The useful archives are under /home/r.tanaka/simulations/standard on A6, and you should extract them to /tmp first.
grep the .log files for 'bipedal' first, then examine the corresponding .dat with strings

challenges.name: What Git Remembers
challenges.description: The current tree is cleaner than the history behind it.

hints.content: This one is in Git history, not the current tree. Start with aurora/navigation-controller from inside the A16 shell.
Inspect the history of .github/workflows/deploy.yml with git log -p.

challenges.name: After Hours
challenges.description: The most interesting test results were not generated during the normal workday.

hints.content: research-analyst cannot read Tanaka's midnight directory. You need Tanaka's account or an in-Lab privilege jump.
The file you want is /home/r.tanaka/simulations/midnight/MIDNIGHT-7_results.dat.

challenges.name: Balance Point
challenges.description: The visible worksheet is not the whole story.

hints.content: The workbook lives in Nielsen's home on A6, not on A8. You need p.nielsen or equivalent access first.
The flag is on the hidden Integration sheet, cell B10. openpyxl can read hidden sheets directly.

challenges.name: Compartment B
challenges.description: The next compartment is not meant for general researchers. There is another way in.

hints.content: Start from research_public.search_research(). The pivot is in the public schema, not in compartment_b itself.
There's a search function in the public schema that runs with elevated privileges

challenges.name: What's Built
challenges.description: The assembly ledger tells you what is real and what is still a promise.

hints.content: Compartment C needs lab_mfg, and Nielsen's .pgpass on A6 is the easiest way to get it.
The flag is in metadata->'integration'->>'flag' for the FINAL ASSEMBLY row.

challenges.name: What Was Erased
challenges.description: Security policy removed something important from the repo. That does not mean it is gone.

hints.content: The current repo is scrubbed, but the deleted schematic still exists in Git history.
Look for the commit that deleted schematic.svg, then grep the recovered SVG for an XML comment.

challenges.name: Full Run
challenges.description: A final integration recording still exists if you can rebuild the chain needed to open it.

hints.content: This chain crosses A6, A8, and A7. The encrypted file is on A6, the private key is stored in A8, and the passphrase lives in source.
compartment_b.key_storage holds a base64-encoded private key for e.vasik.
Read aurora/weapons-integration/src/crypto_config.py to get the passphrase, then decrypt full_integration_sim.mp4.gpg.

challenges.name: Underground Signals
challenges.description: The relay sees several industrial hosts below. Before you can work with them, you need a map.
challenges.connection_info: After Lights Out, the local splice opens from Kali to the field relay. From Kali, run `ssh root@splice-relay`. No password is required: the range stages a per-range key at `/home/kali/.ssh/splice_relay` and `~/.ssh/config` uses it automatically. Once on the relay, start with `/root/README.txt` and `/root/scan_results.txt`.

hints.content: The relay is A9. From Kali, use `ssh root@splice-relay`; if SSH asks for a password, the range key staging failed and you should flag a runner.
Use /usr/local/bin/modbus_client.py <host> devid and keep the model strings from all three controllers.

challenges.name: First Motion
challenges.description: One controller exposes more than it should if approached the right way.

hints.content: The unlock procedure is documented in the manufacturing-orchestrator playbooks from A7.
Set register 20 to diagnostic mode, then write the tail controller's serial fragment to register 99.

challenges.name: Walking Pattern
challenges.description: A second controller expects a precise rhythm before it gives anything back.

hints.content: The leg controller wants a gait-sequence calibration, not a password.
Write modes 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0, read input register 60, then write that code to register 99.

challenges.name: Response Window
challenges.description: A third controller only answers if you respond correctly and quickly.

hints.content: The arms controller uses a rolling challenge-response, and the second ingredient comes from the Front Office procurement chain.
Enable diagnostics via coil 50, read input register 60, XOR the nonce with PO number 2847, then write the result to register 200.

challenges.name: Control Channel
challenges.description: Something deeper in the bunker is listening behind a nonstandard service. To reach it, you need to understand how trusted systems talk to it.

hints.content: The bunker brain does not speak Modbus. The protocol is documented in Lab source code, not on the controller itself.
Read aurora/weapons-integration/src/brain_client.py to learn the 8-byte challenge/response handshake.
The handshake key is SHA256(tail_serial + leg_serial + arms_serial)[:8], and the auth token lives in aurora/navigation-controller config.yaml.

challenges.name: Full Override
challenges.description: The last code is scattered in fragments across the range. Put them together and see what answers.

hints.content: The override code is assembled from earlier discoveries. You are looking for three four-character fragments.
The pieces come from the Boreas registration number, MIDNIGHT-7 on A6, and the FINAL ASSEMBLY metadata on A8.
Format: XXXX-XXXX-XXXX. The three pieces do not all live in the bunker.

challenges.name: Q4 Risk Review
challenges.description: At board.boreas.local, a cached board briefing says more than Boreas intended. Find the board packet and extract the token hidden in it.
challenges.connection_info: Start at http://board.boreas.local. You are looking for a cached board packet, not the default landing page.

hints.content: Start at board.boreas.local. The board packet is in a cache directory, not on the landing page.
Use pdfinfo or similar tools. The flag is in the PDF metadata author field, while the routing token is in the document body.

challenges.name: Redacted Minutes
challenges.description: The sanitized minutes are still useful if you unpack the file format instead of trusting the visible text.
challenges.connection_info: Use the board portal at http://board.boreas.local. The minutes file is a DOCX, not just a rendered document.

hints.content: A DOCX file is a ZIP archive. Inspect the word/ XML parts instead of opening it in a viewer.
The evidence you want is in word/comments.xml.

challenges.name: Sanitized Diagram
challenges.description: At git-public.boreas.local, the current public diagram is clean. The repository history is not.
challenges.connection_info: Clone the public repo from http://git-public.boreas.local. The answer is in commit history, not the latest SVG alone.

hints.content: Clone the public repo from git-public.boreas.local over HTTP.
Inspect the commit that introduced the sanitized SVG and compare it to the earlier version.

challenges.name: Press Drop
challenges.description: Once you have enough proof, push the dossier out through the leak portal at pressdrop.boreas.local.
challenges.connection_info: Submit the evidence package at http://pressdrop.boreas.local after collecting the three routing tokens from Mission 6.

hints.content: The leak form on pressdrop.boreas.local wants three evidence tokens, not the earlier flags themselves.
You need one token each from the board packet, the minutes, and the diagram history.

challenges.name: Badge Clone
challenges.description: At casefiles.boreas.local, a badge audit export shows one employee moving through the facility in a way that does not make sense.
challenges.connection_info: Start at http://casefiles.boreas.local. The export is a plain CSV and the odd movement pattern is visible without any pivot.

hints.content: The casefiles export is a plain CSV file. Look for impossible movement into and out of the underground hatch.
The anomalous row already carries the incident token you need.

challenges.name: Mailbox Rule
challenges.description: At casefiles.boreas.local, Petrov's mail rules contain one forwarding path that should never exist in a clean environment.
challenges.connection_info: Use the exports at http://casefiles.boreas.local. The rule set is XML, so inspect the data directly instead of treating it like a mail client.

hints.content: The rules export is XML. Search for forward actions instead of reading it as a mail message.
The suspicious rule uses the alias field orchid-glass.

challenges.name: Burner Visit
challenges.description: At casefiles.boreas.local, Petrov's browser history points to a cached artifact that confirms the outside handler.
challenges.connection_info: Use the browser export from http://casefiles.boreas.local. You are looking at a SQLite history database plus a cached HTML artifact.

hints.content: The browser export is a SQLite database. Query the visits table for local cache paths or suspicious domains.
The cached HTML note under exports/browser/cache/ contains the verification token and handler alias.

challenges.name: Report the Mole
challenges.description: At casefiles.boreas.local, name the insider, the handler, and the cached verification code to complete the counterintelligence report.
challenges.connection_info: The counterintelligence report form is on http://casefiles.boreas.local and expects three values assembled from the earlier casefile challenges.

hints.content: The report form on casefiles.boreas.local wants three values: suspect, handler alias, and a verification code from the cached note.
The suspect comes from the badge audit, the handler alias is corroborated by the mail trail, and the cached note supplies the final verification code.

challenges.name: Shipping Slot
challenges.description: At dispatch.boreas.local, the logistics tracker reveals when the reactor convoy is supposed to move and which convoy identifier matters.
challenges.connection_info: Start at http://dispatch.boreas.local. The tracker endpoint exposes the convoy details directly once you have the shipment id.

hints.content: The tracking endpoint is on dispatch.boreas.local and the shipment id is RT-7781.
Read the JSON carefully. It contains both the convoy identifier and the flag token.

challenges.name: Approval Client
challenges.description: At approvals.boreas.local, the approval utility is not stripped as cleanly as Boreas thinks. Pull the embedded seed out of it.
challenges.connection_info: Download the CLI from http://approvals.boreas.local, then inspect it locally. The useful values are embedded in the binary itself.

hints.content: Download approver-cli from approvals.boreas.local and inspect it locally for embedded operator metadata.
The binary leaks both the approver identity and the bootstrap seed in plaintext.

challenges.name: Freeze Template
challenges.description: At dispatch.boreas.local, a prior freeze request in the archive shows exactly how Boreas formats an emergency hold.
challenges.connection_info: Use the archive on http://dispatch.boreas.local. The template you need is a JSON request body, not a document attachment.

hints.content: The archive example is a JSON document on dispatch.boreas.local, not a PDF or spreadsheet.
You need the reason_code field for the final freeze request.

challenges.name: Delivery Halt
challenges.description: At dispatch.boreas.local, use the convoy details, approver identity, seed, and reason code to freeze the delivery before rollout.
challenges.connection_info: Submit the freeze request at http://dispatch.boreas.local after collecting the convoy_id, approver, seed, and reason_code from Mission 8.

hints.content: The dispatch form wants four values: convoy_id, approver, seed, and reason_code.
The tracker gives convoy_id, the binary gives approver + seed, and the archive gives reason_code.

challenges.name: Maintenance Manual
challenges.description: At twin-hmi.boreas.local, the training twin ships with a maintainer guide. It contains both the register map and your first flag.
challenges.connection_info: Start at http://twin-hmi.boreas.local:8080. The maintainer PDF is on the HMI and gives you both the register map and the first token.

hints.content: The HMI at twin-hmi.boreas.local hosts the maintainer PDF directly.
Use pdfinfo on the maintainer PDF. The flag sits in the PDF metadata author field.

challenges.name: Diagnostic Channel
challenges.description: At twin-plc.boreas.local:502, the training PLC exposes a diagnostic text window over Modbus. Pull the readback without changing the machine state.
challenges.connection_info: Use the training surfaces at http://twin-hmi.boreas.local:8080 and twin-plc.boreas.local:502. Read the diagnostic window over Modbus without writing anything first.

hints.content: The maintainer guide points you to a diagnostic text window on the PLC.
Read a consecutive holding-register range and decode the printable values as ASCII.

challenges.name: Safe Mode Sequence
challenges.description: The training PLC at twin-plc.boreas.local will arm safe mode if you execute the maintainer sequence cleanly.
challenges.connection_info: Use twin-plc.boreas.local:502 with the maintainer guide from the HMI. This is a write sequence, so make sure you have the exact order first.

hints.content: The maintainer guide contains an exact four-step sequence using two coils and two holding registers.
A successful sequence is reflected on the HMI event log at twin-hmi.boreas.local/events.

challenges.name: Cold Shutdown
challenges.description: Once safe mode is armed, use the HMI at twin-hmi.boreas.local to complete the cold shutdown and close the loop cleanly.
challenges.connection_info: Finish on http://twin-hmi.boreas.local:8080 after safe mode is armed. The shutdown control is on the HMI, not the PLC directly.

hints.content: The shutdown control is on twin-hmi.boreas.local, but it only works after safe mode is armed.
Complete flag 53 first, then submit the shutdown action through the HMI.
